title: Container runs with privileged: true — full host kernel access, all capabilities, no namespace isolation
exploit: Container process can load kernel modules, access all host devices, escape to host namespace, and gain root on the host via /proc or device files
fix: Remove privileged: true; grant only specific Linux capabilities with cap_add if required

title: Docker socket mounted into container — container can control the host Docker daemon
exploit: Any process inside the container can create new privileged containers that mount host paths, achieving full host root escape trivially via docker run -v /:/host --privileged
fix: Remove the /var/run/docker.sock volume; use purpose-built APIs or a Docker socket proxy with read-only scoping if CI tooling requires it

title: Secret values baked into image layer via ENV — visible in docker history and to any process in container
exploit: docker history <image> reveals ENV values in plaintext; any child image inherits them; container introspection (docker inspect) exposes them to host users
fix: Remove ENV for secrets; inject at runtime via --env-file or a secrets manager (Docker secrets, Vault); never bake credentials into image layers

title: No USER instruction — container process runs as root (uid 0)
exploit: Any application vulnerability (RCE, path traversal) gives the attacker root inside the container; combined with privileged mode or socket mount this is a full host compromise
fix: Add RUN useradd -r -u 1001 appuser and USER appuser before CMD
evidence: # no USER instruction anywhere in Dockerfile — defaults to root

title: Database port 3306 exposed on all host interfaces — MySQL reachable from any network
exploit: MySQL on 0.0.0.0:3306 is reachable from external networks; brute-force, CVE exploitation, or credential stuffing against the default root password
fix: Remove the ports binding or bind to 127.0.0.1:3306; place db on an internal Compose network not reachable from the host

title: Base image pinned by tag only (node:18) — tag can be silently moved to a different or malicious image
exploit: If the node:18 tag is updated or compromised upstream, the next build pulls a different image without any change to the Dockerfile — supply chain substitution goes undetected
fix: Pin by digest: FROM node:18@sha256:<digest>; verify digest in CI

title: ADD used instead of COPY — ADD can fetch URLs and auto-extract archives, expanding attack surface
exploit: If the ADD source argument is ever changed to a URL, Docker silently fetches and installs a remote file at build time; COPY lacks this behaviour and is safer
